Chapter 10: Data-Driven Causal Modeling

Inference and Intervention | BK Advisors

BK Advisors

2026-02-12

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Distinguish between causal models and probabilistic models (and why it matters)
  • Explain Markov equivalence and observational indistinguishability
  • Apply constraint-based (PC algorithm) and score-based structure learning
  • Use instrumental variables to identify causal effects from observational data
  • Avoid common regression pitfalls: bad controls, collider bias, and mediator adjustment

Chapter Overview

Causation vs.
Probability
→
Markov
Equivalence
→
Structure
Learning
→
Instrumental
Variables
→
Causal
Regression

The final frontier: Throughout Chapters 1–9, we assumed we knew the causal structure (the DAG). This chapter asks: What if we don’t? Can we discover causal structure from data?

Causation vs. Probability

Two Fundamentally Different Questions

Statistical / Probabilistic:

“What is the joint distribution of staffing, equipment, and mortality?”

\[P(\text{Staffing}, \text{Equipment}, \text{NMR})\]

  • Summarizes associations in data
  • Symmetric: P(A|B) tells us about P(B|A) via Bayes’ rule
  • Says nothing about what happens under intervention

Causal:

“If we increase staffing, what happens to mortality?”

\[P(\text{NMR} \mid do(\text{Staffing} = \text{Adequate}))\]

  • Encodes mechanisms generating data
  • Asymmetric: cause → effect, not reverse
  • Tells us what happens under intervention

The fundamental problem: Multiple causal structures can produce the same joint probability distribution. Data alone cannot always distinguish them.

Three DAGs, One Distribution

Consider three variables: Staffing (S), Equipment (E), Mortality (M)

DAG 1: Chain
S → E → M 		DAG 2: Fork
S ← E → M 		DAG 3: Reverse Chain
S ← E ← M

All three DAGs encode the same conditional independence:

\[S \perp\!\!\!\perp M \mid E\]

Observational indistinguishability: If two DAGs encode the same set of conditional independencies, no amount of observational data can distinguish them. They are Markov equivalent.

When Can Data Distinguish DAGs?

Colliders are the exception. Consider:

S → E ← M

This collider (converging) structure encodes a different independence:

\[S \perp\!\!\!\perp M \quad \text{(marginally independent)}\] \[S \not\perp\!\!\!\perp M \mid E \quad \text{(conditionally dependent)}\]

Key insight: Colliders create a unique independence pattern. If we observe that S and M are marginally independent but become dependent when conditioning on E, we can rule out chain and fork structures. The data identifies the collider.

Markov Equivalence

Equivalence Classes

Markov equivalence class: The set of all DAGs that encode the same conditional independencies. Represented by a CPDAG (Completed Partially Directed Acyclic Graph) — a graph where:

  • Directed edges (→) appear where all DAGs in the class agree on direction
  • Undirected edges (—) appear where DAGs in the class disagree on direction

Example with 4 MNH variables:

Staffing, Equipment, Quality of Care, NMR

If data reveals:

  • Staffing ⊥ NMR | Quality
  • Equipment ⊥ NMR | Quality
  • Staffing ⊥⊥ Equipment (dependent!)

Then the CPDAG has:

  • Staffing → Quality (or —)
  • Equipment → Quality (or —)
  • Quality → NMR (directed)

What we can learn: Quality → NMR is identifiable (directed in all equivalent DAGs).

What we cannot: Whether Staffing causes Equipment, Equipment causes Staffing, or neither — this remains ambiguous from observational data alone.

Implications for MNH Analysis

Practical warning: When we discover a causal model from DHIS2 data, we learn an equivalence class, not a unique DAG. Some edges will be directed (certain causal claims), others undirected (ambiguous direction).

To resolve ambiguity, we need:

  1. Domain knowledge — Experts tell us that training causes competency (not vice versa)
  2. Temporal ordering — Past variables cannot be caused by future variables
  3. Experimental data — Randomized trials identify specific edges
  4. Instrumental variables — Natural experiments that break symmetry

The strategy for Beginnings Fund: Learn the skeleton from DHIS2 data (which variables are connected), then use domain expertise and temporal ordering to orient ambiguous edges.

Structure Learning

Two Approaches to Learning DAGs

Constraint-Based (PC Algorithm)

Idea: Test conditional independencies in data, build skeleton from results

Steps:

  1. Start with complete undirected graph
  2. For each pair (X, Y): test if X ⊥ Y
  3. Remove edge if independent
  4. For each pair (X, Y): test if X ⊥ Y | Z for each Z
  5. Continue with larger conditioning sets
  6. Orient edges using collider rules

Pro: Statistically principled, fast for sparse graphs Con: Sensitive to errors in independence tests

Score-Based (Hill Climbing)

Idea: Search over DAG space, optimize a scoring function

Steps:

  1. Start with empty (or random) graph
  2. Propose adding, removing, or reversing an edge
  3. Compute score (e.g., BIC, BDe)
  4. Accept change if score improves
  5. Repeat until no improvement possible

Pro: Robust to individual test errors, handles small samples Con: Can get stuck in local optima

The PC Algorithm in Detail

Complete
graph
→
Remove
edges
→
Find
v-structures
→
Orient
remaining
→
CPDAG

Edge removal: For each pair X—Y, search for a set S such that X ⊥ Y | S

  • If found, remove edge and record S as the separating set
  • Start with |S| = 0 (marginal independence), then |S| = 1, 2, …

V-structure detection: For each unshielded triple X—Z—Y (no edge X—Y):

  • If Z is NOT in the separating set of X and Y, orient as X → Z ← Y (collider)

Edge propagation: Apply Meek’s rules to orient remaining edges without creating new colliders or cycles.

Score-Based Learning: BIC

Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC):

\[\text{BIC}(G) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \left[ \log P(X_i \mid \text{Pa}_G(X_i)) - \frac{d_i}{2} \log n \right]\]

Where \(d_i\) = number of parameters for node \(i\) and \(n\) = sample size.

Interpretation:

  • First term: fit — how well the DAG explains the data
  • Second term: complexity penalty — penalizes extra edges
  • BIC balances fit and parsimony automatically

In practice: Hill-climbing with BIC is the most commonly used structure learning method. The bnlearn package implements this as hc().

Assumptions and Limitations

Both methods assume:

  1. Causal sufficiency — No unobserved common causes (hidden confounders)
  2. Faithfulness — The data distribution is faithful to the true DAG (no exact cancellations)
  3. Correct functional form — For constraint-based: independence test matches data type
  4. Sufficient sample size — Especially for constraint-based methods

Violation in MNH data: Causal sufficiency is almost certainly violated — DHIS2 data doesn’t capture community-level factors, cultural practices, or household income. Learned DAGs should be treated as hypotheses, not ground truth.

Instrumental Variables

The Endogeneity Problem

Suppose we want to estimate: Does midwife density cause lower NMR?

Midwife Density → NMR

Problem: There’s a confounder (district wealth) that affects both:

Midwife Density → NMR
District Wealth

(District Wealth → Midwife Density AND District Wealth → NMR)

Naive regression of NMR on Midwife Density gives a biased estimate because wealthier districts have both more midwives AND better outcomes for other reasons.

The IV Solution

Instrumental Variable (IV): A variable Z that:

  1. Relevance: Z is correlated with the treatment (Midwife Density)
  2. Exclusion: Z affects the outcome (NMR) only through the treatment
  3. Independence: Z is independent of the confounders (District Wealth)

MNH Instrument: Distance to nearest training college

Distance to
Training College → Midwife
Density → NMR
placeholder District
Wealth

Why this works: Distance to training college affects how many midwives work in a district (relevance), but doesn’t directly cause neonatal deaths (exclusion), and isn’t driven by district wealth (independence — colleges were placed for historical/political reasons).

Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS)

Stage 1: Predict treatment from instrument

\[\text{Midwife}_i = \alpha + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Distance}_i + \epsilon_i\]

Get predicted values: \(\widehat{\text{Midwife}}_i\)

Stage 2: Regress outcome on predicted treatment

\[\text{NMR}_i = \gamma + \delta \cdot \widehat{\text{Midwife}}_i + \nu_i\]

The coefficient \(\delta\) is the causal effect estimate.

Why it works:

  • Stage 1 extracts the part of midwife variation that’s driven by distance (exogenous)
  • This purges the wealth-driven (endogenous) variation
  • Stage 2 uses only the “clean” variation → unbiased estimate

Intuition: We’re comparing districts that differ in midwife density because of training college proximity — not because of wealth.

IV Assumptions: When They Fail

Assumption Violation Example Consequence
Relevance Distance barely predicts midwife density Weak instrument → imprecise, biased estimates
Exclusion College towns also have better hospitals IV estimate captures hospital effect too
Independence Colleges built in wealthy districts IV is confounded → biased like OLS

In practice: The exclusion restriction is untestable — it relies on domain knowledge. The relevance condition is testable via the first-stage F-statistic (rule of thumb: F > 10).

Regression with Causal Awareness

Good Controls vs. Bad Controls

Not all control variables improve your estimate. Some make it worse:

Good Controls

Confounders: Variables that cause both treatment and outcome

Z → X → Y
Z → Y

Controlling for Z removes the backdoor path and reduces bias.

Bad Controls

Mediators: Variables on the causal path

X → M → Y

Controlling for M blocks the causal path and underestimates the total effect.

The Collider Bias Trap

Controlling for a collider creates spurious association:

Staffing → Quality Rating ← Equipment

  • Staffing and Equipment are independent (neither causes the other)
  • Quality Rating is a collider — caused by both
  • If we condition on Quality Rating (e.g., “among facilities rated Good”)…

Collider bias: Among good-quality facilities, those with low staffing must compensate with better equipment. This creates a negative association between Staffing and Equipment — an artifact of the conditioning, not a real causal relationship.

Rule: Never control for a variable that is a descendant of the treatment unless you have a specific reason (e.g., you want the controlled direct effect, not the total effect).

Summary: Control Variable Decision Guide

Variable Type Relationship to X→Y Control? Why
Confounder Z → X, Z → Y Yes Blocks backdoor path
Mediator X → M → Y No* Blocks causal path; underestimates effect
Collider X → C ← Y No Opens spurious path; creates bias
Instrument Z → X, Z ⊥ Y|X Use in IV Use for 2SLS, not as control
Precision variable Z → Y only Yes Reduces variance without bias

*Unless you want the controlled direct effect, not the total effect

The key principle: Use your DAG to determine which variables to control. Regression without a causal model is flying blind — you might adjust for something that increases bias rather than reducing it.

MNH Application: DHIS2 Data

The DHIS2 Data Opportunity

DHIS2 (District Health Information System 2) is used in 8 of the Beginnings Fund’s 10 target countries.

Available variables:

  • Facility staffing levels (monthly)
  • Equipment inventories (quarterly)
  • Delivery counts by facility
  • C-section rates
  • Neonatal outcomes (live births, stillbirths)
  • Referral patterns
  • Drug stock-out frequency

The question:

Can we use DHIS2 data to discover the causal structure linking facility characteristics to neonatal outcomes?

Sample: ~3,000 facilities across 10 countries, monthly data for 5 years = 180,000 facility-months

Candidate Causal Variables

For structure learning, we select 8 key variables:

Variable Type Source
Staffing Adequacy Binary (Adequate/Low) DHIS2 HR module
Equipment Index Continuous (0-1) DHIS2 inventory
Drug Availability Binary (Stocked/Stockout) DHIS2 logistics
Training Completion Binary (Yes/No) Program records
C-Section Rate Continuous (%) DHIS2 delivery data
Referral Rate Continuous (%) DHIS2 referrals
Delivery Volume Count DHIS2 delivery data
NMR Rate per 1,000 DHIS2 outcomes

Expected Causal Structure (Expert Prior)

Based on domain knowledge, we expect:

Training Drug Availability
↓ ↓
Staffing → C-Section Rate
↓ ↘ ↓
Referral Rate → NMR

Goal: Compare this expert-elicited DAG with the data-driven DAG from structure learning. Where they agree → high confidence. Where they disagree → investigate further.

R Workshop: Structure Learning

R Block 1: Simulate DHIS2-Like Data

library(bnlearn)
library(tidyverse)

set.seed(42)
n <- 3000  # facilities

# True causal structure (known to us, not the algorithm)
true_dag <- model2network(paste0(
  "[Training][DrugAvail][Equipment]",
  "[Staffing|Training]",
  "[CSection|Staffing:DrugAvail:Equipment]",
  "[Referral|Staffing:Equipment]",
  "[Volume|Staffing:Equipment]",
  "[NMR|CSection:Referral:Staffing:DrugAvail]"
))

# Simulate from known structure with realistic parameters
training    <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.6)
drug_avail  <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.7)
equipment   <- rnorm(n, 0.5, 0.15) |> pmin(1) |> pmax(0)
staffing    <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.4 + 0.3 * training)
csection    <- 5 + 8 * staffing + 3 * drug_avail + 4 * equipment + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
referral    <- 15 - 5 * staffing - 3 * equipment + rnorm(n, 0, 4)
volume      <- 50 + 30 * staffing + 20 * equipment + rnorm(n, 0, 15)
nmr         <- 35 - 8 * staffing - 4 * drug_avail - 3 * (csection > 10) -
               2 * (referral < 10) + rnorm(n, 0, 5)

dhis2 <- tibble(
  Training = factor(training, labels = c("No", "Yes")),
  DrugAvail = factor(drug_avail, labels = c("Stockout", "Available")),
  Equipment = equipment,
  Staffing = factor(staffing, labels = c("Low", "Adequate")),
  CSection = csection,
  Referral = referral,
  Volume = volume,
  NMR = nmr
)

head(dhis2)

R Block 2: PC Algorithm — Learn Structure from Data

# Constraint-based structure learning: PC algorithm (stable version)
# Uses conditional independence tests to discover edges

learned_pc <- pc.stable(dhis2, test = "mi-cg", alpha = 0.05)
# "mi-cg" = mutual information test for conditional Gaussian (mixed data)

# Compare learned vs. true structure
cat("True DAG edges:\n")
print(arcs(true_dag))

cat("\nLearned CPDAG edges:\n")
print(arcs(learned_pc))

# Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) — lower is better
shd_score <- shd(learned_pc, true_dag)
cat("\nStructural Hamming Distance:", shd_score, "\n")

# Which edges were correctly identified?
true_arcs <- arcs(true_dag)
learned_arcs <- arcs(learned_pc)
cat("\nTrue positives:", nrow(merge(true_arcs, learned_arcs)), "\n")
cat("False positives:", nrow(learned_arcs) - nrow(merge(true_arcs, learned_arcs)), "\n")
cat("False negatives:", nrow(true_arcs) - nrow(merge(true_arcs, learned_arcs)), "\n")

R Block 3: Score-Based Learning (Hill Climbing)

# Score-based structure learning: Hill Climbing with BIC
learned_hc <- hc(dhis2, score = "bic-cg")
# "bic-cg" = BIC for conditional Gaussian networks

# Compare all three structures
cat("=== Structure Comparison ===\n\n")

cat("PC algorithm edges:", nrow(arcs(learned_pc)), "\n")
cat("Hill climbing edges:", nrow(arcs(learned_hc)), "\n")
cat("True DAG edges:", nrow(arcs(true_dag)), "\n\n")

cat("SHD (PC vs. true):", shd(learned_pc, true_dag), "\n")
cat("SHD (HC vs. true):", shd(learned_hc, true_dag), "\n")

# Visualize all three
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
graphviz.plot(true_dag, main = "True DAG", shape = "ellipse",
              highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", fill = "#E24A3F", col = "#E24A3F"))
graphviz.plot(learned_pc, main = "PC Algorithm (CPDAG)", shape = "ellipse",
              highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", fill = "#E24A3F", col = "#E24A3F"))
graphviz.plot(learned_hc, main = "Hill Climbing (BIC)", shape = "ellipse",
              highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", fill = "#E24A3F", col = "#E24A3F"))

Interpreting Structure Learning Results

What we typically find:

  • Skeleton (which nodes are connected) is usually well-recovered with n > 1000
  • Edge direction is harder — many undirected edges in CPDAG
  • Collider structures are the best-identified patterns
  • Long chains are hardest (each intermediate test adds error)

For Beginnings Fund:

The learned structure tells us which DHIS2 variables are causally connected. But the direction of some edges requires expert input.

Strategy: Use learned skeleton + expert orientation + temporal ordering to build the final causal model.

R Block 4: Instrumental Variable Estimation

library(ivreg)  # for 2SLS estimation

# Does midwife density cause lower NMR?
# Instrument: distance to nearest training college

set.seed(123)
n_districts <- 500

# Simulate IV scenario
wealth        <- rnorm(n_districts, 50, 15)           # confounder
distance      <- runif(n_districts, 5, 200)            # instrument
midwife_density <- 20 - 0.05 * distance + 0.3 * wealth + rnorm(n_districts, 0, 3)
nmr_district  <- 40 - 0.4 * midwife_density - 0.2 * wealth + rnorm(n_districts, 0, 5)

iv_data <- tibble(
  NMR = nmr_district,
  Midwife = midwife_density,
  Distance = distance,
  Wealth = wealth
)

# Naive OLS (biased — omits wealth)
ols_naive <- lm(NMR ~ Midwife, data = iv_data)

# OLS with control (correct if we observe wealth)
ols_controlled <- lm(NMR ~ Midwife + Wealth, data = iv_data)

# 2SLS IV estimation (doesn't need wealth)
iv_est <- ivreg(NMR ~ Midwife | Distance, data = iv_data)

# Compare all three
cat("=== Causal Effect of Midwife Density on NMR ===\n")
cat("True effect:        -0.40\n")
cat("Naive OLS:         ", round(coef(ols_naive)["Midwife"], 3), "\n")
cat("Controlled OLS:    ", round(coef(ols_controlled)["Midwife"], 3), "\n")
cat("IV (2SLS):         ", round(coef(iv_est)["Midwife"], 3), "\n")

# First-stage F-statistic (test instrument strength)
first_stage <- lm(Midwife ~ Distance, data = iv_data)
cat("\nFirst-stage F-stat:", summary(first_stage)$fstatistic[1] |> round(1), "\n")
cat("(Rule of thumb: F > 10 for strong instrument)\n")

R Block 5: Good vs. Bad Controls in Regression

# Demonstrate: controlling for a mediator UNDERESTIMATES the total effect
# Demonstrate: controlling for a collider CREATES spurious association

set.seed(456)
n <- 2000

# --- Mediator example ---
# True DAG: Training → Competency → NMR
training_m   <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)
competency   <- 0.3 + 0.4 * training_m + rnorm(n, 0, 0.15)
nmr_m        <- 35 - 15 * competency + rnorm(n, 0, 4)

# Total effect of training (correct)
cat("=== Mediator Example ===\n")
cat("Total effect (no mediator control):",
    round(coef(lm(nmr_m ~ training_m))["training_m"], 2), "\n")
# Controlled direct effect (blocks causal path!)
cat("Direct effect (controlling mediator):",
    round(coef(lm(nmr_m ~ training_m + competency))["training_m"], 2), "\n")
cat("True total effect: -6.0\n\n")

# --- Collider example ---
# True DAG: Staffing → Quality ← Equipment; Staffing ⊥ Equipment
staffing_c   <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)
equipment_c  <- rnorm(n, 0.5, 0.2)
quality      <- 0.3 * staffing_c + 0.4 * equipment_c + rnorm(n, 0, 0.1)

cat("=== Collider Example ===\n")
cat("Staffing-Equipment correlation (unconditional):",
    round(cor(staffing_c, equipment_c), 3), "\n")

# Condition on collider — creates spurious association
high_quality <- quality > median(quality)
cat("Staffing-Equipment correlation (among high-quality):",
    round(cor(staffing_c[high_quality], equipment_c[high_quality]), 3), "\n")
cat("Expected: ~0 unconditional, negative conditional (collider bias)\n")

# Visualize collider bias
tibble(Staffing = staffing_c, Equipment = equipment_c,
       Quality = ifelse(quality > median(quality), "High", "Low")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(Staffing), y = Equipment, fill = Quality)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("High" = "#83BD00", "Low" = "#E24A3F")) +
  labs(title = "Collider Bias: Conditioning on Quality of Care",
       subtitle = "Among high-quality facilities, staffing and equipment appear negatively correlated",
       x = "Staffing (0 = Low, 1 = Adequate)", y = "Equipment Index") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

Putting It All Together: The MNH Workflow

1. Learn
PC/HC on
DHIS2 data
→
2. Orient
Add expert
knowledge
→
3. Validate
Test implied
independencies
→
4. Estimate
IV or adjusted
regression
→
5. Decide
Use for
intervention

This workflow combines data-driven discovery (Ch10) with expert knowledge (Ch2-3), quantitative modeling (Ch4-5), and decision analysis (Ch7-8). The entire book’s toolkit comes together here.

Key Takeaways

Data-Driven Causal Modeling

  1. Data alone cannot determine causation — Markov equivalence means multiple DAGs fit the same data. Domain knowledge is essential to resolve ambiguity.

  2. Structure learning discovers candidate DAGs — PC algorithm (constraint-based) and hill climbing (score-based) recover the skeleton; colliders are the key to orientation.

  3. Instrumental variables overcome confounding — When you can’t control for confounders, a valid instrument recovers the causal effect via 2SLS.

  4. Not all controls are good controls — Controlling for mediators underestimates effects; controlling for colliders creates bias. Let the DAG guide your regression specification.

Looking Ahead: The Complete Toolkit

What you now have:

Chapter Tool
1–3 Qualitative DAGs
4–5 Bayesian networks & inference
6 Causal reasoning pitfalls
7 Decision analysis & do-calculus
8 Resource allocation under uncertainty
9 Multi-agent game theory
10 Data-driven discovery

The Beginnings Fund analyst’s workflow:

  1. Build DAG from expert interviews (Ch2-3)
  2. Quantify with DHIS2 data (Ch4-5, Ch10)
  3. Avoid causal reasoning traps (Ch6)
  4. Make allocation decisions (Ch7-8)
  5. Anticipate strategic responses (Ch9)
  6. Validate and refine with new data (Ch10)

Course Summary

The central message of Inference and Intervention:

Causal models are not just academic abstractions — they are practical tools for answering the questions that matter most in global health: Where should we invest? What should we do? How do we know it will work?

The combination of qualitative modeling (structure), quantitative analysis (parameters), and decision theory (action) provides a complete framework for evidence-based intervention design.

For the Beginnings Fund: This toolkit enables rigorous allocation of $525M across 10 countries, with clear reasoning about why each investment is expected to save lives — and honest acknowledgment of what we don’t know.

Inference and Intervention | Chapter 10
